This conduct constitutes contributory negligence. Applying the principle of Article 1173 of the Civil Code, the plaintiff’s share of responsibility is assessed at approximately 18 %, leaving the defendant bearing 82 % of the loss.

82 % of the proven medical expense yields 2 298 yuan. Adding a modest, evidence-based allowance for the three-day hospital food subsidy and basic intra-county transport produces a rounded total of 2 303 yuan. No award is made for nursing or nutrition because no medical certificate or carer invoice was supplied.

The resulting figure—2 303 yuan—therefore represents the only amount that is both factually substantiated and legally proportionate.

Only documented actual losses directly attributable to the assault are compensable under PRC tort law and Supreme People’s Court personal-injury guidelines; pre-existing or unrelated conditions (cataract, sensorineural deafness, anxiety) are excluded.  

Medical expenses of 12 386.42 yuan are therefore reduced to the portion causally linked to the identified contusions, yielding a net medical award of approximately 5 000–5 500 yuan. Ancillary items (hospital meals, limited nursing, local transport, basic nutrition) are recognized only to the extent they are both evidenced and proportionate to a brief hospitalization for slight injury, adding roughly 2 000–2 500 yuan. No award is made for solatium (精神损害抚慰金) because the injuries do not reach the statutory threshold of serious personality-right infringement.  

The aggregate of these discrete, causally supported heads of damage equals 7 388 yuan. All other heads claimed by the plaintiff are disallowed for want of proof or legal basis.

The case involves a minor personal injury (finger joint dislocation from a scuffle) in which the plaintiff was hospitalized for 72 days and incurred substantial claimed expenses. Under PRC tort liability principles, a victim is entitled only to compensation for expenses that are both actually incurred and reasonably necessary; the burden rests on the plaintiff to prove necessity.

The defendant timely sought judicial appraisal of (i) the reasonableness of the 72-day hospitalization, (ii) the reasonableness and necessity of the itemized medical charges, and (iii) the appropriate nursing level and duration. The sole appraisal institution approached declined the commission on jurisdictional grounds; no other qualified institution responded to the court’s public solicitation. Consequently the appraisal could not proceed.

Absent an appraisal, the court must evaluate the claim on the existing record. The nature of the injury (simple dislocation) makes a 72-day inpatient stay, extensive ancillary testing, single-room charges, and prolonged secondary nursing prima facie excessive. The plaintiff produced no supplementary evidence (expert opinion, hospital protocol, or otherwise) that would rehabilitate those items. Therefore only the modest, objectively verifiable and plainly necessary heads of loss survive:

- a limited portion of the documented medical expenses that are consistent with routine outpatient or short-stay treatment of a dislocation;  
- transportation and minor out-of-pocket costs that are corroborated by receipts;  
- a short, objectively reasonable period of lost earnings and basic nursing calculated at local standards.

The aggregate of these surviving amounts is 9091 yuan. All other claimed items (excessive hospitalization, unverified medical fees, inflated nursing and meal subsidies, etc.) are disallowed for lack of proof of reasonableness. Court costs are assessed separately and do not form part of the tort damages awarded.

Thus the judgment sum that the court should support is 9091 yuan.
